coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of surface molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor for the sensitive quantification of chlortetracycline with ionic liquid and MWCNT improving performance
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yanran
Zhao, Faqiong
Zeng, Baizhao
coredata.dc:description: Chlortetracycline (CTC) is a widely used broad-spectrum antibiotic, its residue likely occurs in the environment and foods, bringing some negative effects to human health. Hence the detection and quantification of CTC in environmental and food samples is relevant. Herein, a novel electrochemical sensor based on surface molecularly imprinted polymer (SMIP) was constructed for the quantitative detection of CTC. The SMIP was synthesized by using ionic liquid (IL) functionalized MWCNT (MWCNT-IL) as supporter, 1-carboxymethyl-3-vinylimidazolium bromide (IL1) as functional monomer, CTC as template, ethylene glycol dimethyl acrylate as crosslinker, and azobisisobutyronitrile as initiator. The obtained composite IL1-SMIP exhibited high adsorption capacity for CTC and the imprinting factor was ca. 4.1. It was found that IL played an important role in improving the property of SMIP, which was also evaluated by DFT-based calculation. The resulting sensor IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE showed high selectivity, sensitivity and reproducibility. CTC could be quantified from 0.4 μM to 55 μM with a detection limit of 0.08 μM (S/N = 3) under the optimized conditions. The practical applicability of the sensor was demonstrated successfully by determining CTC in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Surface molecularly imprinting
Ionic liquid
Chlortetracycline
Electrochemical sensor
Multi-walled carbon nanotube

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of MIP and nonimprinted polymer (NIP)
Computer simulation and independent gradient model (IGM) analysis of CAVImBr and CTC in MIP
Voltammetric response of CTC at different electrodes

The response of IL
1 -SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE sensor to CTC
Imprinting factors, selectivity, reproducibility and stability
Determination of CTC in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors appreciate the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21775112 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized structures of CAVImBr (A) and CTC (B), and the IGM isosurface color map of CAVImBr and CTC (C). Light gray, gray, blue, red and green balls represent hydrogen, carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and chlorine atoms, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
FTIR spectra of MWCNT-COOH (a), MWCNT-IL (b) and [HOEMIM][BF4] (c).
SEM images of IL1-MIP before elution (A), eluted IL1-MIP (B), IL1-NIP (C), MAA-SMIP (D), IL1-SNIP (E, G), IL1-SMIP (F, H).
TEM images of IL1-SMIP (A) and IL1-SNIP (B).

EISs of bare GCE (a), MWCNT-IL/GCE (b), IL1-SMIP (before elution)/MWCNT-IL/GCE (c), IL1-SMIP (after elution)/MWCNT-IL/GCE (d), IL1-SMIP (after rebinding)/MWCNT-IL/GCE (e) in 0.10 M KCl containing 5.0 mM K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6. Inset: the enlarged curve b.

Linear sweep voltammograms of IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE (a), MAA-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE (b), IL1-MIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE (c) and IL1-SNIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE (d) in 0.10 M PBS (pH 6.5) containing 10 μM CTC. Scan rate: 50 mV s−1; accumulation time: 10 min (under open circuit).

(A) DPVs of CTC at IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE, CTC concentration: 0.40, 2.0, 4.0, 6.0, 8.0, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55 and 60 μM (from a to p). (B) The calibration curve for CTC. In 0.10 M PBS (pH 6.5); scan rate: 50 mV s−1; accumulation time: 10 min (under open circuit).

(A) Response currents of IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE and IL1-SNIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE to different substances. Target concentration: 10 μM, other conditions as in Fig. 6 (B) (B) Influence of coexistent substances on the response current of IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE to CTC. Solution composition: 8.0 μM CTC in the presence of 10-fold excess of ciprofloxacin, BPA, DA, cortisol, lomefloxacin. Other conditions as in Fig. 6.
The synthesis route of MWCNT-IL (A) and CAVImBr (B). Schematic diagrams of the preparation of surface imprinted polymer on MWCNT-IL (C) and sensor (D).
Comparison of some previous works with this work.
Determination of CTC in chlortetracycline hydrochloride eye ointments by using IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE and HPLC.
Determination of CTC in milk and tap water samples by using IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE and HPLC.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of surface molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor for the sensitive quantification of chlortetracycline with ionic liquid and MWCNT improving performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430072, Hubei Province, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430072, Hubei Province, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chlortetracycline (CTC) is a widely used broad-spectrum antibiotic, its residue likely occurs in the environment and foods, bringing some negative effects to human health. Hence the detection and quantification of CTC in environmental and food samples is relevant. Herein, a novel electrochemical sensor based on surface molecularly imprinted polymer (SMIP) was constructed for the quantitative detection of CTC. The SMIP was synthesized by using ionic liquid (IL) functionalized MWCNT (MWCNT-IL) as supporter, 1-carboxymethyl-3-vinylimidazolium bromide (IL1) as functional monomer, CTC as template, ethylene glycol dimethyl acrylate as crosslinker, and azobisisobutyronitrile as initiator. The obtained composite IL1-SMIP exhibited high adsorption capacity for CTC and the imprinting factor was ca. 4.1. It was found that IL played an important role in improving the property of SMIP, which was also evaluated by DFT-based calculation. The resulting sensor IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE showed high selectivity, sensitivity and reproducibility. CTC could be quantified from 0.4 μM to 55 μM with a detection limit of 0.08 μM (S/N = 3) under the optimized conditions. The practical applicability of the sensor was demonstrated successfully by determining CTC in real samples.

A novel chlortetracycline (CTC) imprinted poly (ionic liquid) (IL1-SMIP) is prepared on the surface of innovative MWCNT-IL and the resulting sensor shows high performance.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines are widely used broad-spectrum antimicrobial drugs [1]. Among the tetracycline group, chlortetracycline (CTC) is not only widely used to treat diseases, but also added in the feeds of livestock and poultry to ensure their health [2]. However, CTC can hardly be metabolized in animals [3], and its residue is most likely discharged in the environment and introduced into the food chain [4]. Moreover, the accumulation of CTC in the human body can lead to sorts of health problems, such as aplastic anemia and myeloid leukemia [5, 6]. Therefore, monitoring and controlling the content of CTC in the environment and foods is very important. Up to now, many assay methods have been exploited for it, such as liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry [7], high-resolution orbitrap mass spectrometry [8], and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [9]. However, these methods are complicated as they require expensive instruments, complex manipulation and complicated sample preparation. In addition, several simple and convenient methods are also established for the sensitive and efficient detection of CTC, including fluorescence [10], photoluminescence [11] and flow injection spectrophotometric method [12]. For example, Ma et al. developed a whole-cell paper strip biosensor to detect CTC by using β-galactosidase as the reporter protein to achieve visual detection. This method was inexpensive and convenient, but the time for color development was long (about 90 min) [13]. Anyhow, it is still very necessary to develop a simple, rapid, cheap and convenient method for the accurate detection of CTC.

Molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) is a kind of polymer with special spatial structures [14], which can combine with the template molecule through complementary interaction to achieve selective recognition [15]. Owing to its simple preparation process, low cost and good property, MIP has been widely used in separation [16] and purification [17], food safety [18], and environmental monitoring [19]. With the development of molecular imprinting technique, MIP-based sensors attract increasing interest [20, 21]. Many MIP-based sensors have been fabricated for the detection of harmful substances in food [22], and disease markers [23]. For example, Nermin reported a molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor for the detection of interleukin-6. The sensor had a low production cost and good reproducibility [24]. Hassan et al. fabricated a fenamiphos imprinted electrochemical sensor by electropolymerization of pyrrole. The preparation process was simple and the detection limit was low [25]. Abdullah et al. constructed a surface plasmon resonance sensor based on core-shell nanoparticles (Ag@AuNPs) and MIP for the detection of etoposide, and it showed high selectivity and sensitivity [26]. Wang et al. reported a molecularly imprinted photonic polymer (MIPP) sensor for the spectrometric detection of CTC. The proposed method was simple and convenient without any sample pre-treatment and expensive instruments [27]. Liu et al. fabricated a MIP sensor for CTC by using o-phenylenediamine as the functional monomer, while [Fe(CN)6]3 −/[Fe(CN)6]4− electrochemical probe was used for quantification. The sensor showed high selectivity and sensitivity, but the accumulation time was quite long (about 30 min) [28].

Besides, MIP generally has low conductivity. When MIP is used to build electrochemical sensors, conductive materials should be introduced. Kim et al. fabricated MIP on the surface of the poly (terthiophene) layer using acrylamide and boronic acid as functional monomers to detect glucose. The introduced conductive poly (terthiophene) improved the sensitivity of the sensor [29]. Zhao et al. synthesized MIP directly on multi-walled carbon nanotube (MWCNT). The resulting sensor showed good performance due to the excellent electrical conductivity and large specific surface area of MWCNT [30]. Furthermore, MWCNT is inexpensive, so it is an ideal supporting material for surface imprinting.

Ionic liquid (IL) has been widely used in organic synthetic chemistry and polymer chemistry etc. as it has many unique advantages [31]. Poly (ionic liquid) (P-IL) has the characteristics of both polymer and IL [32]. Hence, IL-based MIPs are quite promising. Zhu et al. synthesized MIP on graphene by using IL as functional monomer and crosslinker. The obtained MIP showed remarkable electrocatalytic activity and brilliant adsorption capacity to the analyte [33]. It is worth noticing that the specific recognition effect of MIP mainly relies on non-covalent forces, such as hydrogen bond, π-π stack and hydrophobic interactions, which easily suffer from the hydrophilic environment [34]. The introduction of IL can improve such effects and enhance the performance of MIP.

Herein, we prepared a novel surface molecularly imprinted polymer (IL1-SMIP) on IL functionalized MWCNT (MWCNT-IL), using CTC as template, 1-carboxymethyl-3-vinylimidazolium bromide (CAVImBr) as functional monomer, ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) as crosslinker. The resulting composite was used to construct IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE sensor. The obtained sensor demonstrated remarkable analytical performance, and it could be applied to determine CTC in real samples.
The details of reagents and instruments are presented in the supporting information.

The MWCNT-IL was synthesized through an improved condensation reaction method (Scheme 1 A) [35]. Briefly, under nitrogen atmosphere, 1-hydroxyethyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([HOEMIM][BF4]) (0.214 g, 1.00 mmol), N, N′-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) (0.155 g, 0.75 mmol), MWCNT-COOH (0.050 g), and 4-(dimethylamino) pyridine (DMAP) (0.012 g, 0.1 mmol) were dispersed in acetonitrile (5 mL) and allowed to react for 12 h at room temperature, followed by centrifugation and washing with ethanol and deionized water in turn, and then dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of MIP and nonimprinted polymer (NIP)

ce:section.ce:para: The CAVImBr was synthesized following a known procedure (Scheme 1B) [36]. Briefly, in a 100 mL flask, 1-vinylimidazole (1.88 g, 20 mmol), 20 mL acetone, and bromoacetic acid (2.78 g, 20 mmol) were added in turn. After the mixture was stirred for 24 h at room temperature, the white precipitate was washed with ethyl ether and dried under vacuum for 6 h at room temperature. Fig. S1 showed the 1H and 13C NMR spectra of the product and it was proved to be CAVImBr.

The template CTC (0.052 g, 0.1 mmol) was first dissolved in methanol-H2O (V/V = 5:1, 6 mL) under nitrogen atmosphere. MWCNT-IL (0.020 g) and CAVImBr (0.084 g, 0.40 mmol) were then added. After the mixture was stirred for 30 min at room temperature, azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) (0.008 g, 0.049 mmol) and ethylene glycol dimethyl acrylate (EGDMA) (0.555 g, 2.8 mmol) were added. The mixture was sealed in nitrogen atmosphere and heated at 60 °C for 12 h. The product was extracted with methanol-acetic acid solution (V/V = 9:1) for 48 h at 120 °C by the soxhlet extraction method to guarantee the thorough elution of CTC. Then, the product was dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 4 h and it was labeled as IL1-SMIP (Scheme 1C). The NIP was synthesized following a similar procedure but without CTC and it was denoted as IL1-SNIP.

For comparison, several MIPs/NIPs were synthesized, they were named as IL1-MIP (imprinted polymer using CAVImBr as functional monomer), IL1-NIP (nonimprinted polymer using CAVImBr as functional monomer), MAA-SMIP (surface molecularly imprinted polymer using methacrylic acid as functional monomer) respectively.

First, 4 μL MWCNT-IL suspension (2 mg mL−1 in DMF) was dropped on a clean bare GCE and dried. Then 4 μL IL1-SMIP suspension (2 mg mL−1 in DMF) was dropped on it and dried again (Scheme 1D). Thus, IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE was obtained. Similarly, IL1-MIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE, MAA-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE, and IL1-SNIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE were built. In addition, IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-COOH/GCE and IL1-SMIP/GCE were also constructed.

The equilibrium adsorption amounts of IL1-SMIP, IL1-SNIP, IL1-MIP, and MAA-SMIP materials were measured as follows: 20 mg material was added to 20 mL CTC solution (concentration: 10 μM−300 μM). After the mixture was stirred for 12 h at room temperature, it was centrifuged and the supernate was collected and measured by UV–Vis absorption spectrometry. The adsorption amount was calculated based on formula Q = V(C 0-C S)/m, where V represented the volume of solution, C 0 and C S were the concentration of CTC before and after adsorption respectively, and m was the mass of material.
The details for electrochemical measurements are shown in the supporting information.

All quantum chemistry calculations were performed by using the Gaussian 16 C. 01 program. We first used the Genmer module in Molclus software to generate 200 random structures for the complex, then used the GFN2-xTB method to perform geometric optimization of these 200 structures [37]. Isostat in Molclus software was used to classify the calculated structures and energies according to the following rules: the structures with an energy difference of 2 kcal/mol or structural differences of 5 Å were classified into different types. A certain number of structures were screened out. The binding energy between CAVImBr and CTC was obtained by DFT calculation of the structure with the least energy (i.e. the most stable). The B3LYP functional [38] with 6-311 + G(D) basis set [39] was used to calculate the binding energy. Grimme's D3BJ dispersion correction was also used to ensure the accuracy of calculation results [40]. The binding energy was calculated according to the equation: E
bind = E
complex
- (E
partA
+ E
partB
), where E
partA and E
partB represent the energy of CAVImBr and CTC respectively, E
complex is the energy of the complex system, E
bind is the binding energy. To study the weak interactions between CAVImBr and CTC, we used Multiwfn 3.8 to construct the independent gradient model (IGM) analysis [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Computer simulation and independent gradient model (IGM) analysis of CAVImBr and CTC in MIP
ce:section.ce:para: Before the preparation of MIP, it is important to determine whether there are interactions between the functional monomer and template molecule. In this work, the interaction between CAVImBr and CTC is firstly estimated by computer directional simulation and DFT theoretical calculation. According to the calculation results of DFT, EBind is −169.342 kJ/mol, indicating that weak interaction occurs between CAVImBr and CTC. This means that they are suitable for preparing MIP. To further determine the binding sites and the force type between CAVImBr and CTC, IGM analysis is conducted on the complex systems (i.e. six structures with lower binding energy). The optimized conformations of CAVImBr and CTC are shown in Fig. 1 , while other structures are shown in Fig. S2. As blue represents strong attraction (e.g. hydrogen bonding), green represents weak attraction (e.g. van der Waals action, π-π interaction), and red represents strong repulsion (e.g. steric hindrance), the main interaction forces and interaction sites between CAVImBr and CTC can be deduced. Here the main interaction sites are the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, and the main forces are hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking.

Fig. 2 reveals the FTIR spectra of [HOEMIM][BF4], MWCNT-COOH and MWCNT-IL. For the MWCNT-COOH (curve a), the peak at about 1724 cm−1 corresponds to the vibration of CO [42]. As for curve b, the peaks at 1575, 1465, 1172 and 763 cm−1 can be ascribed to the vibrations of CN, CC, C–N and C–H bonds in imidazolium cation, respectively [43]. Besides, the peak at 1054 cm−1 should be attributed to the vibration of BF4 − [33]. MWCNT-IL (curve b) displays similar peaks to those of [HOEMIM][BF4] (curve c) and MWCNT-COOH (curve a), indicating the successful synthesis of MWCNT-IL. Fig. S4 presents the TEM images of MWCNT-COOH and MWCNT-IL. As can be seen, the sidewall of MWCNT becomes filmy due to the masking of IL [44].

The FTIR spectra of IL1-SMIP, IL1-SNIP and CTC were shown in Fig. S3. For the IL1-SMIP (curve c), the peak around 1560 cm−1 was produced by the stretching vibration of the benzene ring skeleton on CTC, and the peak around 2870 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of C–H on CTC (curve d). When the IL1-SMIP was eluted thoroughly (curve b), its spectra was similar to that of IL1-SNIP (curve a).

Fig. 3 displayed the SEM images of MAA-SMIP, IL1-SMIP, IL1-MIP, IL1-NIP, and IL1-SNIP. Compared with MAA-SMIP and IL1-MIP, IL1-SMIP (Fig. 3F) showed more uniform particle size and larger specific surface area. MAA-SMIP (Fig. 3D) exhibited a 3D porous structure with fewer microspheres. Compared with IL1-MIP (Fig. 3B), IL1-NIP (Fig. 3C) displayed a smoother surface. After elution (Fig. 3B), the particle shape of IL1-MIP became more discernible (Fig. 3A). The morphologies of IL1-SMIP and IL1-SNIP were shown in Fig. 3E–H, respectively. IL1-SMIP (Fig. 3F, H) revealed a 3D porous microsphere structure, while IL1-SNIP (Fig. 3E, G) presented a porous lamellar structure. To further explore the structural difference between IL1-SMIP and IL1-SNIP, TEM experiment was conducted. As shown in Fig. 4 , MWCNT-IL was coated by a polymer layer. It's worth noting that the MIP layer was evener than the NIP layer, which implied that CTC could affect the morphology and structure of the polymer to a certain extent.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy experiments (EISs) corresponding to different electrode interfaces were recorded (Fig. 5 ). The clean GCE had an electron-transfer resistance (Ret) of about 200 Ω (Fig. 5a). After being modified with MWCNT-IL, its EIS was almost a straight line (Fig. 5b), indicating that MWCNT-IL had outstanding conductivity. The IL1-SMIP (before elution)/MWCNT-IL/GCE (Fig. 5c) showed a large Ret (about 1100 Ω), but after CTC was eluted, the Ret dropped down to about 500 Ω (Fig. 5d), manifesting that CTC was removed and the vacant sites were in favor of electron transfer. After CTC was rebound, the Ret increased to about 800 Ω (Fig. 5e). It was on account of CTC taking up the recognition sites and hindering the charge transfer.

To study the adsorption performance of the IL1-SMIP, a static adsorption experiment was executed. As shown in Fig. S5, the adsorption amount of CTC changed with its concentration. Under the same condition, the maximum adsorption capacity of IL1-SMIP (curve a), MAA-SMIP (curve b), IL1-MIP (curve c), and IL1-SNIP (curve d) were about 225, 144, 104 and 72 μmol g−1, respectively. It was clear that the adsorption amount of IL1-SMIP was much higher than that of IL1-SNIP, implying the presence of specific binding sites in the MIP. The adsorption amount of IL1-MIP was lower than that of IL1-SMIP because the surface imprinting made imprinted sites more accessible and effective. As expected, the MAA-SMIP adsorbed less CTC than IL1-SMIP, due to that IL could interact with CTC in various ways, such as hydrogen bond and π-π congestion interaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Voltammetric response of CTC at different electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Different sensors (i.e. IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE, IL1-MIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE, MAA-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE, IL1-SNIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE, IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-COOH/GCE, and IL1-SMIP/GCE) were prepared and their voltammetric responses to CTC were compared. As displayed in Fig. S6, CTC exhibited the smallest peak at bare GCE (curve a). After modifying the GCE with MWCNT-COOH, the peak increased thanks to the good conductivity and large specific surface area of MWCNT-COOH (curve b). The peak current of CTC at the IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE (curve c) increased further, which was twice as much as that at MWCNT-COOH/GCE. The reason was that IL not only had excellent conductivity and bonding effect but also could enhance the dispersity of modifier in solvent and its stickiness on GCE.

As mentioned above, the surface imprinted polymer had more effective recognition sites than the bulk imprinted polymer, so the IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE (Fig. 6 a) showed a higher response current than IL1-MIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE (Fig. 6c). In addition, the peak current of CTC on the IL1-SNIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE (Fig. 6d) was much less than that on the IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE (Fig. 6a), meaning the IL1-SMIP had fairly more specific recognition sites. Besides, CTC produced a higher peak on the IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE (Fig. 6a) than on the MAA-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE (Fig. 6b), suggesting that IL facilitated the adsorption and electrode reaction of CTC to certain extent and the MIP based on CAVImBr as functional monomer had some superiority. Therefore, it could be thought IL (as monomer and modifier) and surface imprinting were favorable to the CTC sensing in this case.

Firstly, the influence of MWCNT-IL amount was studied and the results showed that the peak current increased with the volume of MWCNT-IL suspension (2.0 mg mL−1) increasing until up to 4.0 μL (Fig. S7A), then it decreased. This was associated with the change of electrode surface area and Ret as mentioned elsewhere. Finally, 4.0 μL of MWCNT-IL suspension was used to prepare the sensor.

Next, the amount of IL1-SMIP was optimized since it played an important role in CTC sensing. When the concentration of IL1-SMIP was maintained at 2.0 mg mL−1, we observed that the peak current enlarged with the volume of IL1-SMIP changing from 2.0 μL to 4.0 μL (Fig. S7B). Nevertheless, when its volume surpassed 4.0 μL, the peak current began to decrease. It was most likely due to the increase in Ret. Hence, 4.0 μL of IL1-SMIP suspension was chosen to construct the sensor.

The impact of the pH of CTC solution was also examined. It was found that the peak current bit by bit raised with pH rising from 5.5 to 6.5, and then it decreased with pH changing from 6.5 to 7.5 (Fig. S7C). This was associated with the proton-concerned electrochemical reaction and rebinding effect of CTC. CTC mainly existed as anion when pH was higher (its isoelectric point was pH 5.4), which was favorable to adsorption and rebinding, considering the CAVImBr holding positive charge. However, under weakly alkaline conditions, CTC could form an isomer with lactone due to loop opening, thus its complementary interaction with MIP became worse, unfavorable to the identification of CTC. Accordingly, PBS solution with pH 6.5 was designated for adsorption and detection.

With the increase of solution pH, the oxidization peak potential shifted negatively (Fig. S8), which indicated that protons were involved in the reaction and deprotonation accelerated the oxidation reaction. The linear statistical regression equation between the peak potential (Ep) and pH could be represented as E
p = 0.9980–0.0562 pH, R2 = 0.9978, indicating that the amounts of transferred proton and electron were equivalent.

In addition, the enrichment time was optimized by using 10 μM CTC. We found that the peak current increased gradually with prolonging enrichment time from 5 min to 10 min. It arrived at a stable value above 10 min, suggesting the achievement of enrichment equilibrium (Fig. S7D). Although the enrichment time was optimized based on 10 μM CTC, it was similar to that for other concentration levels. Hence, before detection, 10 min incubation was conducted in the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
The response of IL
1 -SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE sensor to CTC
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 A exhibited the derivative pulse voltammograms (DPVs) of CTC under the optimized experimental conditions. It was clear that the peak current increased when the CTC concentration increased. Over the ranges of 0.4–10 μM and 10–55 μM (Fig. 7B) the peak currents and CTC concentration displayed fine linear relationships, and the linear regression equations were I p (μA) = (2.58 ± 0.05) C (μM) + (0.0577 ± 0.03) (R2 = 0.9987) and I p (μA) = (1.32 ± 0.02) C (μM) + (11.8 ± 0.73) (R2 = 0.9980), respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.08 μM (S/N = 3). Compared with the previously reported methods for detecting CTC, the IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE revealed a wider linear range and a lower limit of detection under a shorter accumulation time. The IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE sensor had higher sensitivity and was also convenient for applications (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imprinting factors, selectivity, reproducibility and stability
ce:section.ce:para: DPV responses of CTC and structural analogs on IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE and IL1-SNIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE were compared and the results were shown in Fig. 8 A. The imprinting factors (IF) for CTC, ciprofloxacin, cortisol, lomefloxacin, dopamine (DA), and bisphenol A (BPA) were calculated [47], and they were 4.12, 1.04, 1.03, 1.05, 1.07, and 1.05, respectively. By comparing the IFs of IL1-SMIP and IL1-SNIP, we could find that IL1-SMIP had obvious specific recognition ability to CTC.

To evaluate the selectivity of the IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE, we recorded the electrochemical responses of 8.0 μM CTC in the presence of some analogs including ciprofloxacin, cortisol, lomefloxacin, DA, and BPA (Fig. 8B). Their chemical formula were shown in Fig. S9. We found they did not interfere with the determination of CTC (DPV signal change <5%) when their concentration was 10-fold of CTC. To appraise the reproducibility, five IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE sensors were prepared and 8.0 μM CTC was tested. As a result, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of peak currents was 4.5% (n = 5). Furthermore, the repeatability was also estimated by using the same sensor to detect 8.0 μM CTC solution under the same condition for 5 times. Consequently, the RSD of peak currents was 5.3% (n = 5). After storing for 15 days in a refrigerator, the sensor maintained 97.7% of its original current response. After one month it still kept 93.6% of the initial value. These results revealed the outstanding reproducibility and stability of the IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of CTC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE was evaluated by detecting CTC in chlortetracycline hydrochloride eye ointments, milk, and tap water samples. Before determination, the eye ointment samples (1.0 g) were diluted with 20 mL of petroleum ether. Then they were centrifuged and 0.2 mL supernatant was 100-fold diluted with PBS solution (pH = 6.5) for measurement. The determined concentrations of CTC were 4.86 and 4.94 μM (the labeled concentration of CTC was 4.85 μM), and the recoveries ranged from 97.6 to 103%. The results agreed with those obtained using HPLC (Table 2 ).

As CTC was not detected in milk and tap water samples, these samples were spiked and recoveries were measured (Table 3 ). The recoveries varied from 97.4 to 105%. At the same time, the matrix effect was evaluated and no pronounced effect was observed (Table S1). The results were in agreement with those obtained using HPLC, demonstrating that the detection of CTC by IL1-SMIP/MWCNT-IL/GCE was reliable.

A novel IL-based surface molecularly imprinted polymer was prepared by using MWCNT-IL as supporter. The obtained MIP composite had remarkable adsorption capacity for CTC, and IL played an important role in it. When it was used for constructing CTC electrochemical sensor, the resulting sensor showed remarkable application potential and could be applied to the measurement of real samples. The sensor not only displayed outstanding selectivity, reproducibility, and stability but also required a short accumulation time. The strategy of using IL as functional monomer for preparing MIP craves a new path to improve the performance of MIP sensors.
Yanran Chen: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Faqiong Zhao: Writing – review & editing. Baizhao Zeng: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21775112).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2021.123130.
